speaker: interviewer
text: Leon Wells interview, tape five. Dr. Wells, you were describing the responsibilities of Charchas in the death brigade, and I'd like you to continue about his duties, and what other people were responsible for.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it is that somebody has to be responsible. It was, afterwards, 120 and so on. It is also to divide the people. We had different groups in the death brigade. One group was the digging group. The other group was carrying the corpses to the wood. We laid out wood and burned when there were about 2,000 bodies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --how that was done?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, you carried the dead body, and you laid it down, one-- one order of wood, and afterwards bodies. Afterwards, next wood, afterward, bodies. And so-- and afterwards, when it was high enough at about 2,000 bodies, you put on kerosene over it, and you set it on fire. After the fire was finished--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that take?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took overnight. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A day and a night. And sometimes a little long, sometimes a little-- depending how dry it is, and so on. After the bodies have finished, there was another group that was called ash colony, colony of ashes, that took the ashes over to a place where it was only stone, and people sat down around it with sieves, and sieved out the ashes. And the gold or any valuables were taken out. And if it wasn't something burned completely, it went back to be burned completely to ashes. See, that's why it was called ash colony.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, there were different groups. Normally, to the ash colony, were people a little bit weaker, older-- older, what I mean, 40 years or 35 years. If you were 20 years, you were in the digging group, and if you were 25 years old or 28, you were a carrier. You carried the bodies to this wood thing. It was maybe-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was a square, done about 8 feet by 8 feet, or 10 feet by 10 feet square, and wood, bodies, wood, bodies, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the gold was taken out, and every night, the German SS men came, three people came, and collected the gold of the day. And they took it to the bank or where else. I don't know. But they came in the evening, after work was done. We never worked more than six, eight hours a day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and-- and normally this was. So. Also, the Charchas was in charge of the whole group for the SS. Like in the morning, somebody-- I am reporting 120 people. I'm reporting 99 people, 40 people, and now every group--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they survive? Did the people in the death brigade, did they have a longer survival rate, the people who did this work?

words.text: the people who did this work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, absolutely. But let us say this way. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They all were killed. We survived at the end-- the survival was eight people. We survived the war. But they were-- but they survived there, inside there. It was no shooting, no killing for us, for our group.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived closer to where the burning was being done? You said that you built a separate barracks there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Barracks there. Yeah, tents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how-- how many weeks or months did this un-digging, or digging up and burning continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until it was finished. And very often-- like, for example, the last one that once we moved to-- the last one, we brought in bodies from outside Lwow, from other towns, to be burned there. It was like trucks, like ice trucks here. Like food trucks big. And they had the bodies, and they brought them in to [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. We moved from Janowska to [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] street. And in [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] we were, and we bought it from outside, including these Polish professors.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how many weeks or months do you think it was that you were doing this work?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were doing it for-- it was from June, and November 19th we made the uprising. And afterwards, they still did it. They brought another group.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many bodies do you think you dug up and burned?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, hundreds of thousands. You know, we brought it from all over.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were burning these bodies for months. It had to create smoke. There had to be--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the people in the town know what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: They knew that somewhere there's smoke from this place coming, smoke. Nobody could come close by. There was SS men standing all over. Nobody could-- it was not in neighborhoods where people lived or people did something. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about--

speaker: interviewee
text: They knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the uprising. Whose idea was it? What was the plan?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, we always spoke yes or no. It wasn't that it came, overnight, a smart idea or something. And it was that until Janowska camp-- was filled, people in Janowska camp, we did not do anything. But once they were liquidated, the last group in Janowska came, and we knew it because they brought it to us to be shot. By the way, it wasn't always only un-digging. Groups were also brought there and being shot. And afterwards, we burned these bodies.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you witnessed the shootings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we saw the shooting. We normally sat in our tents. They did not let us out to watch it or anything. We sat in our tents when they brought in-- normally very early in the morning. But nobody could go out or anything. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And theoretically, there was another [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know, really, if it was a law or not-- that we should not be alive more than two weeks. But until you trained somebody, until they did the job hands-on, it took more. So, they never bothered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was it, theoretically, that you shouldn't live more than two weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's no witness one way or another, so after two weeks, we are shot, and the next group will do it, and so on. But it-- but it-- they kept us. We did our job, and then we did not do anything. And not only this but, for example, we had a devil. The man that was at the ashes, burning the bodies, and push off. He was dressed like a devil, with his devil thing. And we called him the devil. And he was-- you know, because he had to keep the fire going, and he kept with his thing to cows.

speaker: interviewer
text: A fork.

speaker: interviewee
text: A fork. A big fork. And he was dressed like a devil with his--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave them that to wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that for their amusement?

speaker: interviewee
text: Amusement. You know, it was-- everything was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any connection-- did you ever go back into the camp at this point? Do you know what the conditions were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Never. Never connection with anything and anybody. We were separated and only kept by ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, tell me about the plan for the escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, at the end, it was really not a big plan. It was very simple. We did not tell only a few people. I am on there because I had the paper and on. I had to-- you see, we had two big-- three. Two big tents, one smaller and one big. Not too big. In the tent, everybody slept. In one tent. In the second tent, only a certain group of people, like Charchas, a certain-- from the ash colony, from the ashes, because they were cleaner. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And also, the guy that kept-- we had like a leather group of two people, but they were a part of these tents. Like, they fix something for the horses, for the SS men, and so on. You know, for their shoes, and so on. It was kept there. And this was this one tent. So the second tent did not even know what's going on. We kept it completely out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in this tent, where I was, we had a nightly discussion, even among us few. But it's still in this tent, not in the other tent. And we let go-- let them know only the last minute what we decided. It wasn't that we-- that anybody could go in and out. And in the tent, it was also that once, after 8 o'clock or 9 o'clock, I don't remember, it was finished. It was-- You had to be quiet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Close the doors and finish. Our tent had a little bit longer to keep the light on, but it was finished. So it was this one tent, [? this ?] that we were deciding that we should try to break out. But that was all. It wasn't how, whom to kill or not to kill, how to do it, which part, but only how the first few people will break out, open the door, go to the SS man. Say to the SS man, I have a piece of gold, that you stole it, you give it to him privately. You know, because sometimes we dealt with them. We did not deal with them very often, but, you know, but we can have it. Did not go to the bank. And he will open the door to take it, and we will hit him over the head and break out. And that was all.

speaker: interviewer
text: It wasn't a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a big plan, and how and what and where to go, and so on. And nobody knew where to go. Nobody knew what he wants to do. Nobody really was so much involved that if we break out, so what do you do next? So, who cares? So, they will kill you. But here, inside, they will kill you one way or another, and you are collaborating with them. So, you break out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this what had happened. We went over. The two SS men, we knew who will be at duty and with whom we can speak much more openly than with the others. You know, after so many months, we have a certain relation. And to take out and give them the hand, and say, here it is. You don't know if [? he'd ?] [? run in ?] [? and ?] [? kill ?] [? it. ?] And he will open to door to take it, and we will hit him over the head and run out, and finish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did it, and when?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we did it. All. All of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who actually spoke to the soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was one to speak to-- because I was inside. I had-- I could-- they-- they trusted that I could take it. It wasn't that I was smart or not, but I could. I used to bring-- not the only this, by this time, November, I used to bring out for them wood to make fires, that they can keep warm in the night. By 10, 11 o'clock in Poland, it's quite cold. It is not [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Indian Summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you say-- so you say, I will-- you take a lot of wood, and you have something in the hand, and said, I want to come out now. And so-- but I was inside, and I was a guy that did for them for fire. One way or another, they ask me. It wasn't-- So you had the opportunity. You were able--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had their trust.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But I did not know anybody. I did not know even where to go. I did-- You know, as I told you the other day, like they [? decide ?] that I'm [? a leader. ?] I did not even know where I am in Lemburg. It's like dropping somebody off in the middle of New York. Where am I? How do you go to this street? How do you go? I did not even know where I am, where to run, in which direction.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell-- tell me what happened the night of the escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went out--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was around? I mean, were there-- were there still-- was there still action going on in Janowska?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. First of all, first of all, there was no more Janowska. And we were, at this time, at [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Street, which is about, let us say, six, eight miles away from Janowska, in the other part of Lemburg. In the woods, even-- you see, you put down these death brigades in a place where there's no people surrounding it. It's completely in an empty place of the city. So, you are going over there, and-- what was your question?

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to hear about the day or night of the escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, I went-- I was in the night. We knew who it was this night.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were going to escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: All of us who wants it and who does not want. Because certain people did not want to escape. For example, I am telling in the book about Goldberg. He said to us, listen, I'm in the forties. I lost my wife and six or seven children. What do I want even to survive or to escape? What for? What is it here? What do I need to escape?

speaker: interviewer
text: You see, if you have to do with these kind of people, and if you say, yes, but you will not collaborate, he says, who cares about anything. He does not care. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It is collaboration that he is saying, who cares about anything? So, they will say you [? made ?] [? up? ?] Who cares what they will say in the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, this is why I'm saying, out of 23, we were the only ones to make this uprising. Nobody else made the uprising. What I found out after the war. I did not know during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and nobody really saw you go out, so you go to some people, they say, what do I care? Like, for example, this that I described that he buried his two daughters. He couldn't care less. You couldn't even sell him the idea. So you have to be double careful, because one, he does not care. He does not want. And number two, if they will torture him, or they will catch him, he will tell, because he does not care about anything in this world. He does not say, fine, I won't tell. So he will die, too, so who cares about him? So tell me the night-- the actual occurrence.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is what I say. I went out to him, and before I knew, even-- I don't-- I think so. I think this what had happened. The people from my tent, that they knew, and from the other tent that they knew, that wanted to do it, were already behind me and pushing me, and I was out. I did not even have a chance. Had the SS would have-- would have looked at it, he would have seen that I'm going with a group of people. You know. But you see, you have to think about it. In these five months, or four months, they had so much trust in us that nothing can happen. They will not do anything. And they called us like the-- the devil. He played, he sang to them, and on, and they believed everything that he said to them. Because they feel-- you get to a point, you know, they are crazy people. They are dogs. Who cares what they say, and so on. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: He sang to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he sang to them. You speak about to tell you, and I never told in the other story. But the other night, I was in the opera. We had the opera singer. The--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the death brigade?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the death brigade. And he always sang 'La Donna E Mobile' to them. And the whole thing. But I remember. I never knew, you know, as a kid, 'La Donna E Mobile.' And he was telling us exactly the story of the opera, about his life, about his daughter. And he even made jokes all the time that he will give us his daughter. She's a beautiful girl. She's in hiding. We never ask where, I don't know. Nobody would say, my daughter's in hiding, because you don't even tell it. But he had, himself, this philosophy of exactly the same as [? 'El ?] [? Rigoletto.' ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Exactly the same story he had himself. He was a jester, the singer. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The daughter that he gives to people. And that was in our camp. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have these kind of daily occurrence that you don't even think-- for a while, I did not think. But I was in the night, in the opera, 'La Donna E Mobile.' I knew the whole 'La Donna E Mobile,' that's where it's coming. Because it remind me. It came back, this whole-- so you say about it, what does. So, you have to be double. It is not here that you organize whom you trust or not. There's not a trust or not a trust. It's not a human-- you don't have anything anymore humane to deal with a normal situation. So what happened when--

speaker: interviewee
text: So, I run out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Did you have to kill the guy, the SS man?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. We've flopped over each other, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] stepped on him. We probably killed him, because we stepped on him, and run. Run over him.

speaker: interviewer
text: All of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: All-- all of us. And they started, right away, to shoot after us. And I run. I don't even know where.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a plan to meet anywhere?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing. Everybody was on his own. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I run, I did not even know where. But near the railroad station, in the one near Lwow, I met this guy, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. His name was [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. It was Moishe Korn. Korn. But his nose was like [POLISH] in Polish, it's a duck nose.

speaker: interviewer
text: A duck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Duck nose. And this his name was. And he was a horse dealer before the war, and he knew the certain Poles. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I met with him, and he somehow thought, being inside, I must have a lot of gold, taking every day, a little. I did not have anything. I didn't care about anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you have with you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you ran away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Simply nothing. So, he called-- said to me, I gave-- and he was in the ash colony, because he was in his late 30s or beginning 40s. You know. And he took us to a man, and this man took us in over the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Juzek Kalwinski. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And Juzek Kalwinski had a wife and children, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and she said to her father-in-law the next day, he took in these Jews. He probably got from them some money, and he went to drink. That he's a heavy drinker. If he does not come home tonight, I'm going to give out the Jews. Let them hang it. You know, it was simply a wife and husband. And the father heard it, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and he had hidden in his basement 22 other-- 21 other Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the daughter-in-law didn't know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, nobody-- the son did not know, either. And he came in the next night and told us a story right in the-- we were in the stables, upstairs in the stable. And I was laying in one place, and [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] was-- Moishe [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] was on the other side. And he told to Moishe [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. They did not even speak to me. You know, a kid. He's a kid. And he told him that this what his daughter-in-law told him, and he wants him to take him to-- to his basement. But he has in the night, like to escape. So the daughter-in-law or his son does not know. They never told his son, because he was a heavy drinker. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the consequences of the heavy drinking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who knows? You know, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he afraid that you'd be turned over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that he will-- you know, if you drink heavy, you sometimes talk out. If you don't know, you don't know. If you know, then heavy drinking can-- and he said, in the night, I will come, and you will escape from here. And I will come next morning and speak to my daughter-in-law and ask about you and save these people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, for two days, they did not get any food. They escaped in the night and run to somebody else. What else can they do? But they will look for us around, and not finding us, finish. Did you know if anybody from the camp was looking for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't know anything. I did not know anything. I did not think about anything. And so we were-- in the night, we walked out with him. He took us to his place. I did not even know where I am. And the funny thing is, in the night, he-- Moishe [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] said, you know, in meantime, he found out that I don't have anything. And he needs support, too, and so on. And he said, in the night he felt-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he told Mr. Kalwinski, he is a baby. Not he is a baby, but he does not know where he is.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the father-- and this old man said to him, how can I leave him here? He looks like a baby. And while he spoke in Polish, everything in Polish, he used the word baby. Only baby. It's English. You know, it's an international name. And so they took us over to his place. Not they took us over, but they took us between us two so that I don't get lost. We'll stop right here.
